video_title: When the Music Stops in Private Markets | Protect the Pile Episode 17 w/ Leyla Kunimoto
gist: Hedgeye PMs and LP-side analyst Leyla Kunimoto argue the retail democratization of private equity via semi-liquid/evergreen funds is a structurally flawed product whose returns are juiced by buying secondary stakes at discounts and immediately marking them to NAV (ASC 820), crystallizing performance fees on day-one paper gains. They warn the liquidity mismatch in evergreen private equity (low asset turnover plus redemption gates) is a bigger latent problem than private credit, and that price discovery only comes 'when the music stops.'

insights.claim: A wave of redemption gating in semi-liquid private credit and PE funds is becoming visible, with most funds capping quarterly redemptions at 5% of outstanding NAV.
insights.speaker: Leyla Kunimoto
insights.so_what: 5% NAV quarterly caps mean trapped capital compounds; watch gate announcements as a leading sign of forced selling and price discovery in alts.
insights.quote: most funds will cap them have the option to cap redemptions at 5% of outstanding NAV. And this is what's happening right now.

insights.claim: Redemptions beget redemptions in semi-liquid funds because gate headlines reach end-investors only after the redemption window (30-65 days before quarter-end) has already closed, pushing them to queue for the next quarter.
insights.speaker: Leyla Kunimoto
insights.so_what: The reporting lag mechanically rolls outflow pressure forward quarter after quarter; expect Q2/Q3 2026 redemption queues to build, not clear.
insights.quote: redemptions kind of beget redemptions, right?

insights.claim: Publicly listed alternative asset managers Ares, Apollo, and Blackstone are all in negative trend and moving back toward their lows per Hedgeye's risk framework.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kent (RPK)
insights.so_what: The listed alts complex breaking trend corroborates the redemption-stress thesis; these names are tradeable proxies for private-market liquidity stress.
insights.quote: all of the public uh private equity and private credit firms, Ares, Apollo, Blackstone, these charts are all now back to negative trend

insights.claim: Q1 2026 marked the first time a large wave of redemptions hit private credit, making the second quarter the key tell for whether outflows persist.
insights.speaker: Leyla Kunimoto
insights.so_what: Treat Q2 2026 redemption data as a checkpoint; continued outflows would validate that the gating cycle is self-reinforcing rather than a one-off.
insights.quote: In Q1, um in Q1 when news first broke about this was this was the first kind of time that we saw a lot of redemptions come in in private credit.

insights.claim: The S&P 500 sits around 7350, up 7.3% year-to-date and 3.5% off its all-time high, but is printing lower highs in Hedgeye's risk range with the Nasdaq flirting with a trend break.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kent (RPK)
insights.so_what: Lower highs plus mega-cap leadership rolling over (Apple breaking trend) signals topping risk; reduce beta into a weakening tape.
insights.quote: lower highs in the S&P risk range. We've kind of crested here for the moment.

insights.claim: Falling gold, oil and commodities alongside a rising dollar and widening high-yield spreads are consistent with a shift toward a Hedgeye Quad 4 environment of decelerating growth and inflation.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kent (RPK)
insights.so_what: Quad 4 favors long dollar, long duration, and defensives while penalizing credit and cyclicals; position for deceleration.
insights.quote: with gold down, dollar up, uh oil down, commodities basically coming down... now you're seeing high yield spreads starting to pick up

insights.claim: High-yield spreads have widened roughly 25-26 basis points since the start of the month to 283bp.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kent (RPK)
insights.so_what: Widening HY spreads from tight levels is an early credit-stress signal; watch as a real-time gauge of risk appetite ahead of private-credit marks.
insights.quote: High yield spreads at 283. Now, they've moved about 25, 26 basis points now since the start of the month.

insights.claim: The 10-year Treasury has joined the 30-year in bearish trend with the 2s10s spread compressing, signaling lower rates or a flattening curve.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kent (RPK)
insights.so_what: Bearish-trend yields support a long-duration tilt and corroborate the growth-deceleration call.
insights.quote: The 10-year joins the 30-year with now being now bearish trend. So, rates are headed lower or flattening the curve.

insights.claim: The dollar continues to strengthen, particularly after Kevin Warsh's press conference, pushing Hedgeye's short-term liquidity measure slightly negative and lifting the VIX into the 'chop' bucket.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kent (RPK)
insights.so_what: A stronger dollar tightening liquidity tends to precede volatility expansion; the VIX rising in trend argues for hedges.
insights.quote: dollar continues to strengthen, especially post Kevin Warsh's um press conference last week, and that has pushed my liquidity measure down

insights.claim: David Swensen's Yale model worked because illiquid asset classes had wide return dispersion and were uncrowded, making illiquidity a byproduct of opportunity rather than a deliberately harvested premium.
insights.speaker: David Salem
insights.so_what: If today's alts lack the dispersion and scarcity that drove Yale's edge, the historical return template will not replicate for new entrants.
insights.quote: illiquidity became the hallmark of the Yale endowment not because he believed in an liquidity premium... but because the asset classes that had the most dispersion happened to be illiquid at the time.

insights.claim: Retail access to private equity is a 'bastardized' version of the 1985 opportunity because the asset class is now crowded with capital chasing deals, eliminating the bargain-pricing and difficult-access conditions that generated returns.
insights.speaker: Leyla Kunimoto
insights.so_what: Crowding compresses forward returns; retail buyers entering now are paying for an edge that no longer exists.
insights.quote: the access that a retail investor has to private equity is the bastardized version of what David Swensen had back in 1985.

insights.claim: Bottom-quartile private equity managers consistently underperform Treasuries, so the strategy only works if you can reliably select top-quartile managers and gain access to them.
insights.speaker: Leyla Kunimoto
insights.so_what: Without genuine top-quartile selection and access, the average alts allocation is dominated by manager dispersion risk and may trail risk-free rates.
insights.quote: the bottom quartile consistently underperforms every underperforms Treasuries at this point, frankly.

insights.claim: Blindly mimicking the Yale model's exposures rather than its pioneering mindset will destroy more capital than it compounds, a warning Salem made in his May 2000 Barron's review of Swensen's book.
insights.speaker: David Salem
insights.so_what: The democratization wave is precisely the blind mimicry Salem warned against; expect capital destruction among late, undifferentiated allocators.
insights.quote: This book will destroy a lot more capital than it compounds or makes because... people will take what they've read about the Yale endowment and just mimic precisely the exposures

insights.claim: Private investing exhibits 'negative convexity to size' because very large allocators cannot deploy needle-moving capital fractions even when they can identify and access top managers.
insights.speaker: David Salem
insights.so_what: Scale is a structural disadvantage in alts; the largest pools (and the largest evergreen funds) face diminishing returns as they grow.
insights.quote: there's a there's a kind of a negative convexity to size as it relates to private investing.

insights.claim: Mid-size and smaller institutions, not just retail, get taken advantage of in private markets because their desperation for access forces them into non-top-quartile managers.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kent (RPK)
insights.so_what: Access-constrained mid-tier LPs are adversely selected into weaker funds; their commitments are a contrarian signal of where capital is being mispriced.
insights.quote: that's actually the ones who get can actually get really taken advantage of here because if they're desperately looking for access

insights.claim: Evergreen private equity funds rely on a sleeve of secondaries to deploy investor capital immediately, avoiding the J-curve and cash drag created by offering investors instant deployment with no capital calls.
insights.speaker: Leyla Kunimoto
insights.so_what: Secondaries are the structural plumbing of the evergreen boom; understanding the sleeve is key to assessing where reported returns actually come from.
insights.quote: what fills that void in the in the in the private equity space in the in in evergreen vehicles is the secondaries.

insights.claim: ASC 820 lets buyers of secondary stakes mark assets bought at a 10-15% discount immediately up to the underlying fund's NAV, generating an instant day-one paper gain.
insights.speaker: Leyla Kunimoto
insights.so_what: Reported early returns of secondaries-heavy evergreen funds are partly an accounting artifact of discount-to-NAV markups, not realized performance.
insights.quote: the accounting rules that allows this to happen is ASC 820 and that allows the buyer of those stakes... to mark up... to the NAV of the underlying

insights.claim: Many fund managers crystallize performance fees off the unrealized day-one gains created by marking discounted secondary purchases up to NAV.
insights.speaker: Leyla Kunimoto
insights.so_what: Investors may be paying carry on paper gains that never realize; scrutinize fee crystallization timing in evergreen fund documents.
insights.quote: A lot of fund managers will crystallize their performance fee off those unrealized gains. And that's where I have a problem.

insights.claim: Faster capital deployment mechanically boosts reported returns in young secondaries funds because each dollar deployed at a 10-15% discount books an immediate markup, but the effect flattens as AUM scales.
insights.speaker: Leyla Kunimoto
insights.so_what: Returns of new secondaries funds are front-loaded and unsustainable; track records from the rapid-deployment phase overstate steady-state performance.
insights.quote: the faster you deploy capital, the higher your your returns... When your AUM is 15 billion and you're deploying half a billion, it has a lesser impact

insights.claim: The same fund stake can be sold multiple times at successive discounts (fund A to fund B), with each buyer re-marking it up to NAV, and even 100% of a fund's equity trading at a discount does not force the underlying manager to lower its valuation.
insights.speaker: Leyla Kunimoto
insights.so_what: Repeated discount transactions are ignored as valuation inputs, so reported NAVs can persistently overstate clearing prices across the chain.
insights.quote: if the entire equity of a fund trades at a discount, 100%... the fund manager does not have to use that as an input into valuation of their fund.

insights.claim: Secondaries will move front and center as a market conversation over the coming quarters.
insights.speaker: Leyla Kunimoto
insights.so_what: Position ahead of scrutiny on secondaries marks and discounts; the topic is likely to drive headlines and possibly regulatory attention.
insights.quote: My prediction is that secondaries is going to come front and center as a conversation in the next in the coming quarters.

themes.theme: Liquidity Mismatch: PE vs Private Credit

insights.claim: Evergreen private equity is a bigger liquidity-mismatch risk than private credit because private credit has natural turnover (interest payments and ~20% annual loan refinancing) that aligns with 5%-per-quarter redemption caps, while private equity does not.
insights.speaker: Leyla Kunimoto
insights.so_what: The consensus worry is private credit, but the structurally worse mismatch is in evergreen PE; focus risk monitoring there.
insights.quote: private credit of all bad things to put into a liquid wrapper, private credit is the least bad because there's natural liquidity and turnover

insights.claim: Some evergreen private equity funds show asset turnover as low as 0.2, meaning almost no cash flow comes in and the only way to meet redemptions is to sell stakes at a discount.
insights.speaker: Leyla Kunimoto
insights.so_what: Near-zero turnover plus redemption demand forces discounted stake sales, the trigger for downward NAV revaluation; screen evergreen PE funds for turnover.
insights.quote: I see funds with 0.2 turnover of assets. So, which means that there's there's no cash flow coming in and the assets are not like exiting.

insights.claim: Private credit's roughly 20% annual portfolio turnover happens to match a 5%-per-quarter (20% annual) redemption cap, so the structure works in theory as long as the cap is not breached.
insights.speaker: Leyla Kunimoto
insights.so_what: Private credit evergreen vehicles are self-funding for redemptions only until demand exceeds 20% annually; the cap is the breaking point to watch.
insights.quote: If your cap is 5% per quarter times four, that's 20%, which is well aligned with the 20% turnover, natural turnover in your portfolio.

insights.claim: A prolonged period of redemption requests would be far harder for private equity managers to work through than for private credit managers given PE's lack of natural cash flow.
insights.speaker: Leyla Kunimoto
insights.so_what: In a stress scenario, evergreen PE is the likely epicenter of forced selling and gate breaches; underweight or avoid.
insights.quote: if there's a period of prolonged redemption requests in private equity, it's going to be much more difficult to work through that backlog

insights.claim: Total private assets are roughly $15 trillion, of which US evergreen/semi-liquid vehicles are only around $600 billion, with the vast majority held in drawdown vehicles immune to these accounting issues.
insights.speaker: Leyla Kunimoto
insights.so_what: The acute mark-up/liquidity risk is concentrated in a ~$600B slice, suggesting a contained but real problem rather than a systemic GFC-scale event.
insights.quote: Private markets in general like private assets, the size of that market is about $15 trillion... those evergreen semi-liquid vehicles are roughly somewhere in the $600 billion range

insights.claim: Price discovery in private markets only occurs when forced selling begins, and stressed managers will accept larger discounts, so reported NAVs hold until 'the music stops.'
insights.speaker: Leyla Kunimoto
insights.so_what: NAVs are stable by convention, not by market test; the catalyst for repricing is a forced-seller event, which is the key risk trigger to monitor.
insights.quote: we dance while the music is playing. Um when the music stops playing, somebody's going to have to start selling assets and that's when price discovery is going to happen.

insights.claim: This is unlikely to be a GFC-style event because illiquid private marks are not as prevalent or mark-to-market in the system, and assets held long enough should eventually exit, even if at less attractive prices.
insights.speaker: Leyla Kunimoto
insights.so_what: The base case is slow-motion underperformance and trapped capital rather than a sudden systemic crisis; size positions for grind, not crash.
insights.quote: GFC-wise I don't I just it's not as as prevalent in the system right now. And the the saving grace will be nobody knows what those things are worth

insights.claim: IRR is fundamentally non-comparable to public-market index returns because it is computed on deployed capital and can be gamed via leverage, delayed deployment, and early capital returns.
insights.speaker: Leyla Kunimoto
insights.so_what: Do not compare advertised PE IRRs to public returns; demand DPI/MOIC and net-of-fee public-market-equivalent metrics instead.
insights.quote: IRR is calculated on the money deployed. It's not it's not the same thing as your returns in the S&P 500 index... It's it's like apples and oranges.

insights.claim: A large asset manager advertising mid-20s IRR since the 1980s is implausible because continuous compounding at that rate would make its size exceed US GDP.
insights.speaker: Leyla Kunimoto
insights.so_what: Headline since-inception IRRs are marketing artifacts; the GDP reductio shows they cannot reflect actual compounded investor wealth.
insights.quote: if that particular asset manager was generating IRR continuously, like they would be generating their size would be greater than the GDP of the United States

insights.claim: The same underlying private company can be carried at starkly different valuations by different VC managers holding identical units, exposing the unreliability of NAV-based marks.
insights.speaker: David Salem
insights.so_what: Identical-asset valuation divergence means audited NAVs carry real model risk; treat single-source private marks with skepticism.
insights.quote: certain VC firms had the same units in the same series of the same private companies valued at starkly different valuations.

insights.claim: Valuation is a trade-off between time and price, so a 40 Act mutual fund subject to daily redemptions can justifiably mark the same private asset lower than a long-hold endowment because it may be forced to sell at an inopportune time.
insights.speaker: David Salem
insights.so_what: Liquid wrappers should logically carry lower marks than long-hold vehicles for identical assets; evergreen funds holding at full NAV are mispricing their redemption obligation.
insights.quote: The valuation of anything... is a trade-off between time and price.

insights.claim: 40 Act mutual funds are permitted to hold up to 15% of assets in illiquid securities.
insights.speaker: David Salem
insights.so_what: The 15% bucket is the regulatory channel through which illiquid private assets reach daily-liquidity retail products; a pressure point if redemptions spike.
insights.quote: the 40 Act mutual funds are permitted to have up to 15% of their assets in illiquid assets

insights.claim: The cleanest fix for the liquidity mismatch is to not hold illiquid assets in liquid wrappers, or to list them on public markets as closed-end funds so investors set a market-clearing price.
insights.speaker: Leyla Kunimoto
insights.so_what: The industry resists listing because daily trading would raise volatility and lower Sharpe ratios, revealing that smooth NAVs are the product's selling point.
insights.quote: the solution to this is don't hold illiquid assets in liquid wrappers. Or list them.
